question_text: Explain why bulbs in a parallel circuit remain equally bright even if one bulb burns out and is removed.
correct_answer: In a parallel circuit, each bulb is on its own separate branch with its own complete path to the battery. When one bulb burns out, it only breaks its own branch, not the other branches. The remaining bulbs still receive the full voltage and current, so they stay equally bright.
explanation: Each bulb in a parallel circuit has its own independent pathway. The voltage across each bulb stays the same (equal to the battery voltage), and removing one bulb doesn't affect the current flowing through the others. This is why parallel circuits are reliable for household wiring.
